coredata.dc:title: Recent advances and developments on integrating nanotechnology with chemiluminescence assays
coredata.dc:creator: Tiwari, Ashish
Dhoble, S.J.
coredata.dc:description: Chemiluminescence (CL) techniques are extensively utilized for detection of analytes due to their high sensitivity, rapidity and selectivity. With the advent of nanotechnology and incorporation of the nanoparticles in the CL system has revolutionized the assays due to their unique optical and mechanical properties. Several CL-based reactions have been developed where these nanoparticle based CL sensors have evolved as excellent prospects for sensing in various analytical applications. This review article addresses the nanoparticles based CL detection system that are recently developed, the mechanisms has been summarized and the role of luminophors have been discussed. This article critically analyzes the optimal conditions for the CL detection along with quantitative assessment of the analytes. We have included the use of semiconductor nanoparticles, metal nanoparticles, graphene based nanostructures, mesoporous nanospheres, layered double hydroxides, clays for CL detection. The scope and application of these nanoscale material based CL system in various branches of science and technology including chemistry, biomedical applications, pharmaceutics, food, environmental and toxicological applications has been critically summarized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Nanoparticles
Applications

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanisms involved in QD assisted CL
Application of CL in nanomaterial characterization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the electron-hole transition resulting in CL. Injection of holes into the CQDs by oxidants increases the holes in the CQDs and accelerates the electrons–hole annihilation resulting in CL emission.

Schematic representation of Ag QDS catalyzing the formation of OH• radical which subsequently react with H2O2 to generate superoxide anion. O2 - accelerate the generation of 1,2‐dioxetanedione which leads to CL.

Proposed hydrolysis pathway for HXTZ in alkaline solution (0.02molL−1 NaOH). The metabolites PT-1–2 & PT-1–3 binds with Au NPs causing decrease in catalytic activity due to aggregation of the Au NPs. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [21]. Copyright 2014 Elsevier.

Schematic representation of the proposed CL assay for propranolol. Interaction of Au NPs with propranolol changes the surface charges of Au NPs, hence subsequently resulting in enhanced CL intensity. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [22]. Copyright 2015 Elsevier B.V.

Resonance energy transfer taking place from RB molecule to the Au NPs. Ferricyanide ion reacts with RB molecule and also get consumed by Au NPs thus decreasing the CL. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [24]. Copyright 2015 Elsevier B.V.

(A) Schematic illustration of the CRET process between the donor, luminol, and the accepter, QDs, based on the immunoreactions between mAb-HRP and BS-QDs. CRET resulted in the increase of fluorescence intensity of BS-QDs; and (B) Schematic illustration of the CRET-based competitive immunoassay for SMZ. SMZ inhibits the binding between BS-QDs and mAb-HRP resulting in decreased CL. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [26]. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society.

Spectra of MSA capped CdTe QDs (a) in the presence of Luminol (b) and Luminol-H2O2. CRET resulted in drastic decrease in the CL intensity. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [43]. Copyright 2016 Elsevier B.V.

CRET from luminol to CdTe QDs: (A) Schematic principle of the CRET with GOx-HRP-CdTe QDs biconjugate; (B) luminol CL spectrum and the absorption spectra of three CdTe QDs (QD545, 2.3nm; QD594, 3.9nm; QD643, 4.4nm); and (C) CRET spectra obtained with GOx-HRP-QDs bioconjugate of three CdTe QDs. QD545 indicates CdTe QDs with maximum emission wavelength of 545nm. The in-situ generation and consumption of H2O2 by oxidase and peroxidase respectively resulted in increased fluorescence quenching of QDs. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [44]. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society.

Schematic illustration of CL mechanism for acidic KMnO4–Au NCs reaction. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [62]. Excited state Mn (II) is the potential luminophor for this system. Copyright 2015 Elsevier B.V.

Schematic diagram for the chemiluminescence detection of caspase-1 based on peptide cleavage technology. Caspase-1 recognizes and cleaves the YVAD sequence presented in peptide, allowing peptide and luminol labeled Au NPs to dissociate from the functionalized magnetic beads. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [68]. Copyright 2015 Elsevier B.V.

Schematic illustration of the enzyme linked CL assay for detection of d-glucose based on dendritic Au–Ag bimetallic nanoparticles. The presence of luminol on their surface due to Au–Nand Ag–N covalent interactions increased the CL intensity. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [96]. Copyright 2016 Wiley.

head.ce:title: Recent advances and developments on integrating nanotechnology with chemiluminescence assays

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Naveen Government College, Pamgarh 495554, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Naveen Government College

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, RTM Nagpur University, Nagpur 440033, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, RTM Nagpur University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemiluminescence (CL) techniques are extensively utilized for detection of analytes due to their high sensitivity, rapidity and selectivity. With the advent of nanotechnology and incorporation of the nanoparticles in the CL system has revolutionized the assays due to their unique optical and mechanical properties. Several CL-based reactions have been developed where these nanoparticle based CL sensors have evolved as excellent prospects for sensing in various analytical applications. This review article addresses the nanoparticles based CL detection system that are recently developed, the mechanisms has been summarized and the role of luminophors have been discussed. This article critically analyzes the optimal conditions for the CL detection along with quantitative assessment of the analytes. We have included the use of semiconductor nanoparticles, metal nanoparticles, graphene based nanostructures, mesoporous nanospheres, layered double hydroxides, clays for CL detection. The scope and application of these nanoscale material based CL system in various branches of science and technology including chemistry, biomedical applications, pharmaceutics, food, environmental and toxicological applications has been critically summarized.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is the luminescence produced due to chemical reaction. It is a well known luminometry technique for detection of analytes and also for being more simple, reproducible, rapid, sensitive and selective [1]. CL based sensors are better than optical sensors because of the very low (absence of) background signal as compared to the absorbance and fluorescence emitted by a light source [2]. CL assays of dynamic range of compounds (drugs, immunoassays, biotechnology, biosensing, food technology, clinical etc) have grown exponentially during the last decade [3,4]. Nanoparticles are fascinating materials having size below 100nm and belong to a class of materials that have been continuously researched since the last two decades. The use of nanoparticles as florescent probes has several advantages over traditional organic luminophors. They have unique size-dependent properties (optical and electronic) and are having excellent analytical potential [5]. Most of the CL based reactions have low quantum efficiencies and hence produce a weak luminescence. Nanoparticles by virtue of their size-dependent and optical properties can be employed as sensitizers and consequently can bring significant enhancement of the weak emission. CL sensors were extensively explored and developed based on the CL behavior of these nanoparticles. These NPs are having excellent characteristics as CL emitters such as large molar extinction coefficients, high quantum yields, surface trap-control luminescence, low photobleaching/photodegradation and are chemically resistant [6]. There are several important reviews on CL that covers the early developments and applications of chemiluminescence in analytical, diagnostics and other allied fields that can be benefitted from chemiluminescence [7–13]. However, chemiluminescent functionalized nanoparticles in present scenario are innovative concept in nanotechnology and gaining more and more attention. Although some very good review articles were published on nanomaterial amplified CL by Li et al. and critical summary of CL reactions involving CRET mechanism along with their biosensing applications by Huang and Ren but no further developments are reported after 2012 [14,15]. These articles throw light on combination of nanomaterials with CRET/CL and emphasize on designing new optical sensors and developing analytical methods for futuristic studies.

We think it is appropriate to provide a detail study to highlight novel developments in this field. We have tried to cover the literature until 2017. In this review article we have critically summarized reaction conditions, relationship between analyte's structure and CL intensity the effect and role of different classes of nanomaterials (metal nanoparticles, nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots, silica NPs, layered double hydroxides, natural and modified clays, ROS induced CL etc.) in sensing, imaging and other applications in various applications. Application of CL in monitoring of basic sites, oxygen vacancies and oxygen states in nanomaterials has also been discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms involved in QD assisted CL
ce:section.ce:para: The unique photophysical properties of QDs particularly size tunability, large stokes shifts, high quantum yield have made them as versatile fluorophores. The excited state of QDs can be produced by photoexcitation, electron injection, electron impact, or chemical reaction. Chen et al. in their review article [16] have excellently explained that QDs can participate in CL reactions as catalysts or energy acceptor, or a direct QDs oxidation and energy transfer taking place simultaneously to enhance the CL intensity. Based on these facts three mechanisms has been highlighted with new examples (for a detail account Chen's article can be referred).

This mechanism involves direct excitation of the QDs which results in generation of electron and hole due to chemical reaction. CL intensity depends on the redox reactions that regulates electron-hole transfer and also on the efficient coupling of these reactions and the QDs. It can be understood on the basis of electron hole injection mechanism. In a redox type of chemical reaction, transfer of electron takes place forming an excited state QD known as electron injection while in an another reaction an electron is scavenged from lower energy level of the QD known as hole injection. The transition of injected electrons from higher energy state to lower energy state resulted in luminescence as shown in Fig. 1.

Amjadi et al. proposed a direct chemiluminescence mechanism for S, N-CQDs induced by some common oxidants. Chemical reaction between S, N-CQDs and KMnO4 results in CL emission as follows.
S , N − CQD +
KMnO
4
→ S , N − CQDs * + Mn
(
II
)
S
,
N
− CQDs * →
S
,
N
−
CQDs
+
h
ν
( ~ 500
nm )
The injection of holes into these QDs by oxidants increases the holes and hence accelerating the electrons–hole annihilation, resulting CL emission [17].

Biparva et al. studied the TCPO-H2O2 reaction in which Ag QDs catalyzed the formation of OH• radical which subsequently react with H2O2 to generate superoxide anion. Superoxide anion accelerates the production of 1,2‐dioxetanedione of the key intermediate, which is important to the TCPO–H2O2 CL reaction as shown in Fig. 2 [18]. In a similar mechanism Au QDs catalyzed the reaction between the luminol radical and oxygen, resulting in an enhancement of the CL in luminol–K3Fe(CN)6 CL system [19]. In case of CuO NPs-HCO3 –-H2O2 CL system, the suggested mechanism involved the reaction of HCO3 – with H2O2 resulting in formation of HCO4 • −. Then, CuO NPs catalyzed the decomposition of HCO4 − to CO3 •− and HO• through the O–O bond homolysis. Finally, CO3 •− recombined to form (CO2)2 *, emitting light [20]. Khajvand et al. suggested a possible mechanism in which Au NPs catalyzed luminol-H2O2 system. When hexithiazox (HXTZ) is mixed with Au NPs, the CL intensity decreased. The suggested mechanism involved HXTZ being hydrolyzed to the metabolites PT-1–3 and PT-1–2 at pH > 7. The binding of Au NPs to PT-1–3 and PT-1–2 resulted in aggregation of Au NPs (as shown in Fig. 3), which reduced catalytic activity of Au NPs in the luminol– H2O2 CL system [21].

Qi et al. studied the catalytic activity of unmodified Au NPs on the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction, and the interaction of unmodified Au NPs with propranolol [22]. The surface charge property of Au NPs directly affects their catalytic performance for CL reaction, and the interaction of Au NPs with propranolol can change Au NPs' surface charge property and induce the Au NP aggregation, and after aggregation the catalytic activity of Au NPs on the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction is greatly enhanced as shown in Fig. 4.

Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) is nonradiative process in which energy transfer takes place from a chemiluminescent donor to a fluorophore acceptor, provided that there must be an overlapping of CL emission spectrum with the absorption spectrum of fluorescent acceptor. Due to the broad spectrum range of QDs they act as excellent fluorescent acceptors in CRET processes.

Du et al. suggested a CRET mechanism for TCPO-H2O2-fluorescein CL system, the excited fluorescein molecules transfer their energy to non-radiation dispersed AuNPs, causing a decrease in CL signal [23]. Vahid et al. reported that in alkaline [Fe(CN)6]3--RB-SDS system a characteristic peak around 580nm has been similar to the fluorescence spectrum of RB in alkaline medium, hence the emitter must be an excited sate intermediate [RB•*]. This excited state molecule, then transfer its energy to the RB molecules and emission occurs [24]. A resonance energy transfer takes place from RB molecule to the Au NPs. Moreover, [Fe(CN)6] 3- can react with RB molecules and also, the oxidant consumption by Au NPs result reducing the CL intensity. This can be explained by Fig. 5. Kang et al. reported a resonance energy transfer process in CdTe/calcein/K3Fe(CN)6 system, where the energy donor CdTe QDs transfer energy to the acceptor calcein by dipole–dipole interaction [25]. Ma et al. synthesized hapten functionalized QDs (BS) for the development of a CRET-based competitive immunoassay to detect sulfamethazine (SMZ) in milk samples. The carboxylic group of hapten BS was conjugated to the amino group of the QDs using carbodiimide and the catalyst, HRP, was linked to the specific antibody (mAb4D11) resulting in mAb-HRP. The mechanism involves the specific binding of mAb-HRP to the BS-QDs. When p-iodophenol was added to the luminol (donor)-H2O2, CRET can occur resulting in enhancement of fluorescence intensity of BS-QDs as shown in Fig. 6 [26].

In CL systems involving nanomaterials having size > 10nm (other than QDs) the CL enhancement can be ascribed due to their catalytic activity in the electron transfer process. Li et al. studied the effect of different shapes of Au NPs on the luminol based CL reaction and the results indicated that the NPs having more polar sites (tadpole shaped or other irregular shaped) had 100 fold more catalytic efficiencies as compared to the spherical particles [27]. In case of CL systems involving dendrimers, a bimolecular interaction between the high-energy intermediates and fluorophores resulted in decreased CL intensity [28].

Size dependent optical and electronic properties of semiconductor nanoparticles have been utilized in various analytical applications. These nanocrystalline particles have high reactivity and tunable band gap, hence can serve as excellent CL emitters. Poznyak et al. were probably the pioneer workers to investigate band gap chemiluminescence of CdSe/CdS core shell and InP nanocrystals in solution and in nanoparticulate layers [29]. QDs system was utilized by Chen et al. as a chemiluminescence probe to determine L-ascorbic acid in human serum. The results indicated that CL intensity and the concentration of L-ascorbic acid had a good linear relationship in the ranges of 1.0 × 10−7–1.0 × 10−4 mol/L and detection limit (LOD) for L-ascorbic acid was 6.7 × 10−9 mol/L [30].

Chang et al. prepared mercaptophenylboronic acid (MBA) capped Mn2+-ZnS QDs in aqueous solution at room temperature as a biosensor for the detection of transferrin (TRF) as the model glycoprotein [31]. The results suggested that a significant fluorescence enhancement of MBA@QDs was observed in the presence of TRF through boronate-affinity. The electron transfer process from the QDs to boronic acid moieties got blocked effectively upon the addition of the TRF resulting in a loss of quenching efficiency and an enhancement in fluorescence intensity. The MBA@QDs can sensitively detect down to 5.69 × 10−9 M. TRF in solution and the linear range is 0.10–10.0μM.

QDs-based sensors are gaining importance in pharmaceutical and biomedical analyses. Pawar et al. reported for the first time CdS QDs-based fluorescence probe for the determination of dolasetron mesylate (DM) used as an antinauseant and antiemetic agent [32]. The results obtained by Stern-Volmer studies indicated that there is a static quenching due to formation of ground state complex between the QDs and DM which is also favored due to electrostatic interaction between them. The results indicated that there was a quenching of fluorescence intensity of QDs with an increasing concentration of DM. It was found to be linear in the range of 2–40μg/mL and LOD being 1.512μg/mL. Satisfactory results were obtained when this method was applied to detect DM in human urine and blood serum sample. Zhou et al. found that CL intensity of luminol-KIO4 system was catalytically enhanced by thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots in alkaline media. The effect of KIO4 on the CL intensity suggested that at optimum concentration KIO4 decomposes to produce some radical intermediates (such as O2 •− and OH•) that accelerated the oxidation of luminol. The system was utilized to determine methionine in commercial pharmaceutical formulations with recoveries between 98.0% and 101.9% [33].

Azizi et al. developed CL analysis system based on L-cystein capped ZnS:Mn QDs and used for chemiluminometric determination of folic acid in pharmaceutical formulation [34]. It was found that when the ionic liquid 1,3-dipropylimidazolium bromide/copper complex was introduced to the system, a drastic light emission enhancement was observed due a strong interaction between Cu2+ and the imidazolium ring. The results suggested a linearity in the range of 1 × 10−9–1 × 10−6/pM of folic acid with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9991 and LOD being 1 × 10− 10 M.

Zhou et al. used l-cysteine capped Mn-doped ZnS@Si QDs as a sensitizer to enhance chemiluminescence (CL) signals of NaClO-H2O2 system under basic condition [35]. The CL enhancement of NaClO–H2O2 was due to the catalyzed decomposition of H2O2, producing reactive intermediates hydroxyl radical (.OH) and superoxide anion (O2 −) that subsequently formed the oxygen excimer species (1O2)2 * and through a rapid electron-transfer process it returned to the ground state. Now, the excited sensitizer returns to the ground state resulting in CL enhancement. Khataee et al. observed that the CL intensity of KMnO4–carminic acid system catalyzed by CdS QDs was quenched in the presence of a trace level of cloxacillin [36]. The results suggested that the decreased CL intensity had a good linear relation with the cloxacillin concentration in the range of 0.008–22.0mgL−1. The detection limit (3σ) was found to be 5.8µgL−1. A CL system based on the reaction between KMnO4–CdS QDs reaction catalyzed by cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) in acidic medium was developed as a CL probe for the sensitive determination of atenolol by Khataee et al. [37]. The result indicated a linear relationship in a range of 0.001–4.0mgL−1 and 4.0–18.0mgL−1, with a detection limit (3σ) of 0.0010mgL−1. The method gave satisfactory results in determination of atenolol in spiked environmental water samples and commercial pharmaceutical formulation. Hosseini et al. found that amino acids histidine and tryptophan catalyzed the CL reaction of CdSe QDs–H2O2 system due to reaction of QDs with hydrogen peroxide. In the optimum conditions, a linear calibration curve in the range of 0.66–35.5μM and 0.83–35.1μM was obtained for histidine and tryptophan, respectively [38].

In another work Hosseini et al. utilized the CdSe QDs–H2O2 system for the determination of dysprosium ions in water samples. The results suggested that optimum concentration of QDs must be maintained as the CL intensity quenches with increasing concentration of QDs. The limited energy generated due to reaction between H2O2 and OH- cannot excite all QDs and hence affect CL efficiency. [39]. Dong et al. very recently found that weak CL of lucigenin obtained in alkaline solution (in the absence of H2O2) could be significantly enhanced by CdS, CdSe, and CdTe QDs (CdS QDs produced the most intense CL)[40]. The enhancement in the intensity was attributed to CRET that could possibly occur due to overlapping of the lucigenin CL spectrum and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdS QDs. This method was satisfactorily applied to determine ascorbic acid in juice samples. Another interesting CRET mechanism was observed by Kaviyarasan et al. for mercaptosuccinic acid capped CdTe QDs (acceptor) catalyzed luminol-H2O2 system (donor) [41].

Zhang et al. observed that horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-mimicking DNAzyme could effectively enhance the CL emission of CdTe quantum dots (QDs)–H2O2 system. Enhancement in CL intensity originated from the catalysis of HRP-mimicking DNAzyme on the CL reaction between CdTe QDs and H2O2. The QDs-based CL system can be utilized as a potential candidate to detect sensitively CdTe QDs and HRP-mimicking DNAzyme in biologic labeling [42].

Kang et al. reported a CL energy transfer process in K3[Fe(CN)6]-calcein system in alkaline medium catalyzed by CdTe. The process involved CdTe QDs as energy donor and the calcein as acceptor. It was found that a good linear relationship existed between the quenched CL intensity and the concentration of polyphenols hence the method was applied for the determination of the polyphenols like chlorogenic acid, quercetin, hyperin, catechin and kaempferol. The CdTe/calcein/K3Fe(CN)6 CL system was applied for determining hyperin in seed of C. chinensis Lam. with satisfactory results [25].

Kaviyarasan observed that addition of MSA (mercaptosuccinic acid) capped CdTe QDs to luminol and luminol-H2O2 system resulted in a considerable reduction in the CL intensity and was accounted by CRET processes [43]. The emitter was the excited state luminol QDs conjugate between luminol and free –COOH group of MSA capped CdTe QDs as shown in Fig. 7.

It was often reported that in most of the luminol-H2O2 based CL system, peroxide could oxidize QDs resulting in quenching of the CL intensity. Xu et al. fabricated an organo-modified hydrotalcite-CdTe nanocomposite (NC) as a CRET probe [44]. In this type of NC the hydrophobic dodecylbenzene sulfonate provided a protective microenvironment to prevent the oxidation of QDs. The oxidase and HRP enzymes bioconjugated onto the surface of QDs resulting in fast in-situ generation and consumption of H2O2 respectively and hence increasing the fluorescence quenching of QDs as shown in Fig. 8. This system was applied for analytical determination of cholesterol, glucose, and benzylamine with LOD being 0.8, 3.4, and 10nM, respectively.

Very recently Sklenářová et al. studied the effect of surface modified (mercaptopropionic, mercaptoundecanoic, thioglycolic acids and mercaptoethylamine) CdTe QDs on permanganate-induced and luminol–H2O2 CL using a sequential injection technique [45]. The results suggested that the CL enhancement was strongly dependent on the concentration of QDs and varied significantly for different surface modified CdTe QDs. CL Enhancement was observed to be more than 120% for QDs having sizes of 2.8nm, 3.3nm and 3.6nm and emitting at 520, 575 and 603nm respectively using the flow-batch detection cell in the stop-flow mode.

Li et al. very recently reported catalytic enhancement of CL intensity of KIO4 by CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs. ESR studies revealed that CL mechanism and it was explained on the basis of radiative recombination of injected holes and electron in which CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs acted as emitter [46].

Zhang et al. recently reported that Cu2−xSe NPs catalytically enhanced the CL in luminol–Cu2−xSe system due to formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [47]. This CL-based method was applied to determine cholesterol in human serum and CL enhancement was proportional to the concentration of cholesterol over the range of 82nM to 1.96μM with LOD being 0.062nM. This study can further stimulate research in the rapid and accurate detection of cholesterol in cholesterol associated diseases like Alzheimer.

A single functionalized nanoparticle can be used to bind a variety of CL reagent molecules. CL functionalized noble metal nanoparticles are limited to Au NPs and other noble metal nanoparticles functionalized by CL have not been exploited due to lack of facile and reliable procedures in their synthesis. He et al. developed an ECL DNA sensor for the detection of M. tuberculosis DNA based on luminol–Ag NPs [48]. The results indicated there is a covalent interaction (Ag–N) between Ag NPs and luminol/aminophthalate ion which enhanced the CL property. The LOD for target DNA was 0.03fmolL−1 (S/N = 3) which is much less than other report [49]. In an another article He at al. reported that amino acids including l-cysteine, l-proline, l-phenylalanine, l-arginine, l-threonine, l-glutamic acid, and l-tyrosine can be identified at a concentration of 10ng/mL using luminol functionalized Ag NPs having a sensitivity of about 3 orders of better magnitude than other reported methods [50]. He et al. developed CL sensor array comprising of luminol-functionalized silver nanoparticle (Lum-AgNP) and H2O2 CL system for the discrimination of organophosphate and carbamate pesticides such as dimethoate, dipterex, carbaryl, chlorpyrifos, and carbofuran [51]. The results indicated that they can be accurately determined at a concentration of 24μg/mL with an accuracy of 95%. The sensitizing effect of berberine (anti cancer drug) on the reaction of TCPO-H2O2 catalyzed by biomimetically synthesized Ag nanoparticles was recently reported by Biparva et al. A linear range from 5 × 10−9 to 5 × 10−7 molL−1 (r > 0.9843, n = 8) was obtained for berberine concentrations with a detection limit of 8.7 × 10−10 molL−1 [18]. In fact, the molar extinction coefficient of Ag NPs is larger than that of Au NPs and Cu NPs and they also demonstrate unique optical properties associated with the surface plasmon resonance (SPR). Energy transfer phenomenon takes place when the emission wavelength of CL reaction and the SPR peak(s) are overlapped. The excited state CL emitter transfers energy to surface plasmons in Ag NPs resulting in enhanced CL. Recently a comprehensive review of the published studies in field of Ag NPs was very well explained by Iranifam [52].

Biomedical studies have emerged as one of the most extensively researched area in field of diagonistics. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have attracted much attention in different bio-affinity assays due to their unique physical and chemical properties, such as easily controllable size distribution, long-term stability, superior conductivity, large surface area, and biocompatibility.

Colloidal AuNPs are excellent material as chemical and biological sensor due to their unique optical and mechanical properties. Sun et al. utilized Au NPs triggered CL in developing an aptasensor to detect platelet-derived growth factor B-chain (PDGF) that is responsible for tumor growth and transformation [53].

Jiang et al. investigated the catalysis of hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme (HGDNAzyme) on the CL of luminol functionalized AuNPs in the presence of H2O2 [54]. They developed an aptasensor for the determination of Interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) a cytokines secreted by immune cell. The study revealed that lum-AuNPs in the aptasensor can perform different functions viz. target recognition, loading and restraining the conformation of the DNAzyme halves for target-recognition-induced modulation of catalysis which enhances CL and to immobilizing luminol molecules for CL signal generation. The result suggested that LOD ( at Signal-Noise ratio S/N = 3), was 0.4nM and hence can detect physiological levels of the IFN-γ.

Dong et al. studied the enhancing effect on the CL reaction of luminol with ferricyanide catalyzed by Au NPs in alkaline solution. CL enhancement was found to be dependent on different sizes of nanoparticles. The CL enhanced for Au NPs for ~38nm and was accounted due to red shift in surface plasma resonance (SPR) absorption band with increasing size of nanoparticles. This method has been successfully applied in the determination of ascorbic acid in several real samples [19].

Another particle size CL dependence of Au NPs was reported by Lin et al. for peroxymonocarbonate (HCO4 -)-eosin Y system [55]. The result suggested that nanoparticles of different sizes can enhance the CL of the HCO4 --eosin Y system, and the most intensive CL signals were obtained with 50nm diameter gold nanoparticles. Interestingly it was found that, for nanoparticles less than 50nm CL intensity decreased suggesting that the adsorption of HCO4 - onto the surface of gold nanoparticles caused partial electron transfer from the Au NPs to the adsorbed HCO4 - known as particle-mediated electron transfer.

Roux et al. synthesized luminol attached gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by virtue of the bridging molecule dihydrolipoic acid [56]. The results showed that the functionalized AuNPs have good electrochemiluminescence (ECL) property. Xing's group reported a method to link tris(2,20-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) to the AuNPs surface by using cysteamine and DNA as a joint molecule, which has been successfully applied to DNA assays [57,58].

Mercaptoaniline-coated gold nanoparticles (Au@MA) grafted onto flat gold surface was fabricated by Adabo et al. and can find a potential application as biochip [59]. This leads to the enhancement of the biodetection signal which rests on the light emitted by oxidized luminol. The biotin-streptavidin recognition event was monitored by the CL of luminol after electrochemical grafting of gold nanoparticles. The Au@MA NPs induced enhancement of the chemiluminescence of luminol can serve as a promising strategy for rendering flat surface more sensitive for monitoring recognition events. Luminol–H2O2 CL reaction for the detection of trypsin based upon the digestion of cytochrome c with trypsin to release heme are incompatible due to strong basic condition of luminol CL reaction and weak basic medium of protease hydrolysis.

You et al. developed very recently a sensitive and label-free CRET platform for the detection of protease by impairing TCPO–H2O2 CL reaction with bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized AuNCs [60]. This strategy can be easily extended to detection other proteases also.

Detection of metal ion sensors particularly for Mn2+ ion detection based on Au NPs have certain limitations and are significantly affected by external environmental factors such as temperature, salts etc. and hence has poor selectivity and accuracy. Very recently Li and He have developed sensors in which Mn2+ enhances CL between luminol-modified AuNPs and AgNO3 [61]. The increased stability and reactivity was accounted on the basis of the mechanism in which in-situ formation of H2O2 reaction of Mn2+and superoxide anion radical (O2•–).

Vahid et al. were the first to report the quenching effect of Au NPs on the CL reaction of Rhodamine B (RB)-ferricyanide [Fe(CN)6]3- system in alkaline media. This was then subsequently restored by the presence of the Hg2+ due to RB release from Au NPs surface and hence can be utilized as Hg2+ sensors in environmental water samples [24].

You et al. investigated direct CL reaction between bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized Au NCs and five classic oxidants, namely KMnO4, Ce(IV), K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2 and N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) [62]. The CL spectrum suggested that the excited state Mn(II) is the potential luminophor for this system as shown in Fig. 9. The method can be applied potentially for the determination of H2O2 in water samples.

A selective electrochemical aptasensor was developed by Deiminiat et al. for the indirect determination of bisphenol A [BPA, 2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane] based on functionalized multiwall carbon nanotubes/gold nanoparticles (f-MWCNTs/AuNPs) [63]. The aptamer was immobilized on the surface of f-MWCNTs/AuNPs nanocomposite film modified gold electrode through the formation of thiol–gold (S–Au) bonds between the gold nanoparticles and the thiol group of the aptamer at 5′ end. The aptasensor was highly selectivity toward BPA molecules with negligible cross-reactivity with resembling congeners such as bisphenol B (BPB), 4,4′-biphenol (BP) and 6F bisphenol A (6F-BFA). It had a good stability, reproducibility and a linear dynamic range from 0.1 to 10nM with a limit of detection of 0.05nM.

Zhu et al. reported for the first time the synthesis of CdSe QDs decorated poly (diallyldimethylammoniumchloride)-graphene (CdSe QDs/PDDA-GN/AuNPs) and applied as probe to assemble an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for detecting ractopamine (RAC). The dual amplification of the sensors was achieved due to AuNPs modified electrode and the CdSe QDs/PDDA-GN/AuNPs as probe [64]. The detection of RAC was found to be in linear range from 0.01 to 1000ngmL−1 and a lower detection limit of 2.6pgmL−1 (S/N = 3).

Ru(bpy)3 2+ functionalized gold nanoparticles were used as thiol-versatile signal probe monitoring activity and inhibition of protein kinase. AuNPs plays dual role by capturing numerous signal-generating molecules and also for recognizing thiophosphorylated peptide, providing a universal detection model for protein kinase catalyzed processes using one single Ru(bpy)3 2+-AuNPs. It was found that by changing the peptide substrate, different target protein kinase can be detected. The versatility of the method suggests that it can be a promising signal probe for multiplex bioassay of protein kinase and also for kinase inhibitor profiling. The ECL intensity was proportional to the activity of Casein kinase II (CK2), CK2 in the range of 0.01–0.5 unit/mL with a low detection limit of 0.008 unit/mL and to the activity of and cAMP-dependent protein kinase (PKA) in the range of 0.01–0.4 unit/mL with a detection limit of 0.005 unit/mL hence can be potentially applied for drug screening [65].

Boro et al. utilized the catalytic properties of Au NPs in the luminol–silver nitrate–gold nanoparticle based chemiluminescence (CL) system for the detection of widely used herbicide 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) [66]. The CL based immunoassay showed the detection limit of 2,4- D in standard water samples around 3ngmL−1. It can be a potentially utilized as a fast screening methodology (< 30min) for pesticide detection.

Very recently Yang et al. found significant CL intensity in fluorescein-H2O2 system catalyzed by Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and subsequently applied in detection of trace level of catechol in environmental water samples [67].

Khajvand et al. observed the quenching effect of the hexithiazox HXTZ in the Au NPs catalyzed luminol–H2O2 system. The CL intensity was found to be linear with HXTZ concentration in the range of 0.017–0.42μgmL−1, and LOD being 0.011μgmL−1. This method gave satisfactory results when applied to the detection of HXTZ residues in citrus fruits and water samples at trace levels [21].

Du et al. found that CL intensity of bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)oxalate-hydrogen peroxide-fluorescein reaction had significantly decreased due to Au NPs because the absorption band of dispersed gold nanoparticles perfectly overlapped with the chemiluminescence spectrum. This method was used to determine melamine contamination in milk samples with satisfactory results [23].

Wu et al. fabricated the chemiluminescence caspase-1(a cysteine protease) assay probe by using peptide, luminol and Au NPs [68]. The carboxyl terminated magnetic beads were linked with peptide and luminol labeled Au NPs forming the probe functionalized magnetic beads (MBs) as shown in Fig. 10. The results indicated that Caspase-1 can be detected in the concentration ranged from 0.01ngmL−1 to 30ngmL−1 with LOD being 0.003ngmL−1.

Zhang et al. had employed CL designing strategy for determination of fibrinogen (Fib) (plasmatic glycoprotein) [69]. They found that aggregated Au NPs had higher catalytic activity on the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction than the dispersed AuNPs. The assay showed linearity in the range of 2.7 × 10−15–2.7 × 10−13 M and LOD for fibrin being 1fM. The size of Au NPs did not only affect the catalytic activity of Au NPs in the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction, but also influenced the Fib–AuNPs binding. The may be accounted on the basis of increased electron density of Au NPs with increasing size.

In most of the researches involving the effect of reaction conditions on the CL intensity using metal NPS size dependent properties of the colloid has been explored. However Aslan and Geddes reported that metal surface plasmons can be directly excited by chemically induced electronically excited molecules providing amplified CL emission [70].

Chen et al. reported for the first time surface-plasmon-assisted enhancement of the ultraweak CL using Cu/Ni metal NPs NaHCO3-H2O2 system. The enhancement in CL attributed to the surface-plasmons-coupled CL of (CO2)2 * emitter generated by chemical reaction of CL reaction of HCO3 - and H2O2 [71]. This system can be further utilized for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in environmental and biological samples. A lateral flow LF-immunoassay with enhanced sensitivity and thermostability was developed by Park et al. using Pt nanoparticles with the peroxidase activity [72]. The immunoassay was applied to the detection of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) and the it was found that the sensitivity improved by 1000-fold compared to the conventional rapid test based on colored gold-colloids. Se NPs–potassium permanganate–dinitrobutylphenol (DNBP) CL system was investigated by Iranifam et al. for the determination of DNBP at ppm level in the water samples of different origins [73]. The CL intensity was found to be in the range of 1.0 × 10−7–8.0 × 10−5 gmL−1 with LOD being 3.1 × 10−8 gmL−1.

The surface modified mesoporous silica nanoparticles MSN by active functional groups can be used for encapsulation/immobilization of a huge amount of payloads including mediators, enzymes, and antibodies. An ultrasensitive CL immunosensor of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) using HRP functionalized MSN was developed by Chen et al. [74a]. The large surface to volume ratio of MSN carriers facilitated high HRP bound per sandwiched immunoreaction, along with enhanced signal intensity and increased sensitivity. The coimmobilization and detection sensitivity of this protocol depended on the ratio of HRP and anti-CEA loaded on the surface of MSN spheres, through a competitive aldehyde–amino coupling reaction, hence amount of HRP and anti-CEA onto nanoparticles has to be optimized for CL enhancement. It is more proficient than traditional sandwich immunoassay as it has limited one or two enzyme molecules per antibody [75]. The results indicated that immunosensors had a high sensitivity, reproducibility and good analytical performance. Chen et al. recently reported an aptamer based MSN controlled release system coupled with the luminol/H2O2 CL assay system to develop a sensitive biosensor for detection of Cocaine [74b]. Cocaine binds with its aptamer with high affinity which subsequently releases the entrapped glucose into the solution. The glucose then react with the dissolved oxygen to produce gluconic acid and H2O2 in the presence of glucose oxidase and, the H2O2 generated in this reaction can enhance the CL of luminol in the alkaline solution. An isoluminol-H2O2-HRP CL system was recently established by Ji et al. On the basis of the inherent cleaving of disulfide bonds in the presence of glutathione (GSH), the CL system of isoluminol-H2O2-HRP, and the signal amplification of periodic mesoporous silica (PMS), the amount of free thiol compounds in cancer cells was detected. The result suggested that a linear dose response in the range from 5 × 10−10 to 8 × 10−8 M. The detection limit of this trial for GSH determined is 5 × 10−10 M. The proposed design was successfully applied to the extracts of K562 cell for intracellular thiols detection, the average amount of thiols was about 4.114 × 10–13 M per K562 cell [76].

Luminescent carbon nanomaterials are now substantially studied in fabricating CL based sensors because of their fascinating physicochemical properties. The carbogenic nanodots have excellent electron donating and accepting properties which make them a potential candidate as a futuristic luminescence material provided that the low quantum yield can be dealt with. Studies so far revealed that doping with elements like N, P or S resulted in increasing the quantum yield but analytical application of these CDs are limited. Lin's group was the first to study the CL properties of CDs in the presence of ONOOH and successfully applied it for nitrite sensing [77].
Very recently Shah and Lin have published an interesting perspective on the recent progress on the CL properties of CDs [78].

CL biosensors require development of fast, cost effective and portable protocols for point-of-care testing (POCT) for accurate determination of trace amounts of analytes. The fabrication of microfuidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) has opened new doors in bio-analytical applications. The CL property of the carbon dots was suggested to be due to the oxidant injected holes and electrons, which gave us new insight into the characteristic of C-dots. Wang et al. designed a DNA sensor N,N′-disuccinimidyl carbonate (DSC) for direct covalent immobilization of DNA strands on μPADs, and carbon dots (C-dots) dotted nanoporous gold (C-dots@NPG) was employed for signal amplification [79]. The detection limit was low as 8.56 × 10−19 M and hence this sensor is ultrasensitive as compared to most of the well known methods.

The effect of hollow fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (HFCNs) and solid fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (SFCNs) on the luminol-H2O2 CL system was studied Wu and Han [80]. This system was applied in determining dipyridamole (a cardiovascular drug) in tablets and urine samples with good results.

Studies suggest that H2O2 can induce ultraweak CL on reacting with certain inorganic compounds but the reactive nature, instability and decomposition of H2O2 has limited its application. Recently Dou et al. reported that FCNs can directly react with luminol, NaHCO3 and NaHSO3 in alkaline conditions and produce CL [81]. The experimental evidences suggested that FCNs are mimics of H2O2 and hence this can open a new avenue for studying ultraweak CL.

A hemin functionalized graphene sheet was fabricated assembling hemin on nitrogen-doped graphene by Deng et al. [82]. The graphene sheet could act as an oxygen reduction catalyst to produce sensitive electrochemiluminescent (ECL) quenching of quantum dots (QDs) due to the annihilation of dissolved oxygen. The hemin@NG shows highly efficient electrocatalytic activity toward the reduction of O2 via a four-electron pathway, which greatly prevents the formation of H2O2 as a QD-based ECL coreactant, and thus quenches the ECL emission of QDs. They used a carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a protein biomarker and this immunoassay method could detect it down to the subpicomolar level with a detection range over 5 orders of magnitude.

Wang et al. reported that cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide passivated carbon nanodots (CTAB-CDs), can be used as excellent catalysts to dramatically enhance the CL intensity of the luminol–H2O2 system [83]. The CDs catalysised the electron-transfer and the breakdown of H2O2 effectively into OH• and O2 •−,and hence 1O2 - participating CL enhancement mechanism was proposed. CL properties of carbon nanomaterials greatly depend on their structure and synthesis procedure. Amjadi et al. investigated the direct chemiluminescence (CL) of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) induced by KMnO4. The injection of holes into the CQDs by KMnO4 increases the holes in the CQDs and accelerates the electrons–hole annihilation, which subsequently enhanced the CKL intensity. It was found that CL intensity of this system was significantly decreased by adding Cu2+ ions which was probably due to the interaction with S,N-CQDs. This inhibiting effect was utilized to design a sensor for the determination of trace amount of Cu2+ in human plasma and water samples [17].

In another work Amjadi et al. reported the CL of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induced by direct chemical oxidation. Results showed that Ce (IV) ions could oxidize GQDs to produce a relatively intense CL emission [84]. The mechanism of CL was accounted due to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. GQDs-Ce (IV) CL system was applied to determine uric acid. A good linearity was observed in the range of 1.0 × 10−6–5.0 × 10−4 M with LOD being 5.0 × 10−7 M.

Carbon nitride (C3N4) is structurally similar to graphene in having a graphite-like stacked 2D structure, which could be regarded as a nitrogen heteroatom-substituted graphite framework. The large surface area and special structure of these nanomaterials can improve the sensitivity and the stability of the CL system as compared to the conventional liquid phase CL. Interestingly metal-free constitution of g-C3N4 renders the materials nontoxic and biocompatible for some biological applications. Tang et al. found that g-C3N4 had intensively long-persistent luminescence property, which was subsequently applied as a turn-on persistent luminescence probe for imaging and detecting biothiols in biological fluids [85]. The results suggested that strong CL when NaClO was injected into the g-CNQDs, and was further utilized detect free chlorine. Free residual chlorine in water had a very wide range of detection from 0.02 to 10μM and LOD being 0.01μM. Recently Song et al. described the recent advances in luminescent sensors based on g-C3N4 CL [86].

Liu and Han recently reported that CL intensity of C-dots-KMnO4 system in acidic medium enhanced on addition of sulfide. At optimum conditions a linear plot was obtained in the range 1.0–100μM sulfide concentrations, with LOD being 0.2μM. This method can be a promising method to determine trace levels of sulfide in water samples and environmental analysis [87].

Shah et al. very recently reported that nitrogen doped carbon dots N-CDs when injected into the Fenton reagent, effectively enhanced the weak CL of the Fenton reaction [88]. These ecofriendly N-CDs played an important role in peroxide-induced redox cycling of Fe2+
↔
Fe3+.

Very recently CuO nanoparticles (CuO NPs)–catalyzed chemiluminescence (CL) system, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)–hydrogen carbonate (HCO3 −)–CuO NPs, was developed by Iranifam and Hendekhale for determination of atorvastatin, rivastigmine and topiramate [20]. It was found that no direct participation of CuO NPs in CL enhancement was observed and they served just as catalysts. In fact, CuO NPs catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 to HO• by the O–O bond homolysis. The good ability of CuO NPs in the catalysis of the CL systems can provides new routes towards developing of new CL-based analytical methods.

Fe3O4 magnetic NPs (MNPs) were synthesized by Kim et al. via co-precipitation method, and the surface was modified by amine group [89]. MNPS offers specific binding of the target molecules through formation of an amide bond. The targeted inhibition of amine capping with alkyl groups can increase the interaction forces of the MNP surface with incoming antibodies through change of the chemical and physical properties, including H-bonding and polarity. A unique drop type (DCL) system used to detect luminescence from the reaction between HRP and the luminol–H2O2 mixture. An amine-targeted inhibition method using propanal was developed for immunoassay and the method was used for the determination of ochratoxin A in spiked rice sample.

Luminol based CL detection system can be coupled to enzymatic reactions thereby increasing the sensitivity and selectivity of the glucose determination. A new glucose biosensor was developed by immobilization of glucose oxidase (GOX) on the Fe3O4–chitosan (Fe3O4–CS) nanoparticles by Chaichi and Ehsani [90]. The detection of signal was performed by luminol- H2O2 CL system and AuNPs catalaysed enzymatic and the CL reactions. The LOD (3σ) was 4.3 × 10−7 M. The proposed method is comparable with the cyclic-Voltammetry technique using gold electrode [91]

Yu et al. synthesized the magnetic composite particles reduction of Au3+ with hydroxylamine in the presence of Fe3O4 particles obtain Fe3O4@Au and a magnetic chemiluminescent enzyme immunoassay for determination of deoxynivalenol (a potential carcinogen) in food [92]. This analytical method had a very low LOD of 0.032ng/mL.

Abdolmohammad-Zadeh et al. found that CL signal of H2O2–luminol system was improved in the presence of CoFe2O4 MNPs and Ag(I) ion had an inhibitory effect on catalytic activity of the CoFe2O4 MNPs [93]. Based on these facts they fabricated a chemosensor for the detection of Ag(I) ion in several water samples having a LOD of 0.15ngmL-1 Ag(I) ion. Coupling of MNPs with immunoassay are very effective due to the fact that the mass-transfer distance of analytes and reagents to the immobilized antibody on the MNPs is significantly decreased and also due to its intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

Zhang et al. developed a magnetic particles-based (CLEIA) for detection of serum GPC3 (sGPC3) [94]. MNPs with carboxyl group were modified with streptavidin, and further used as solid phase for coating GPC3 antibodies. This system was proposed for detection of the promising hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) tumor markers GPC3. The cut- off value of sGPC3 in diagnosis of HCC was determined at 21.01ng/mL. The results confirmed that sGPC3 could well differentiate HCC patients from ones with normal liver and secondary liver cancers.

Alarfaj and El-Tohamy synthesized gelatin based composites containing bimetallic Au–AgNPs and employed it as catalytic agent in a CL luminol–potassium ferricyanide system for detection of anticancer Raloxifene hydrochloride (RLX) [95]. A linear relationship in the range of 1.0 × 10−9–1.0 × 10−1 mol/L was obtained with a limit of detection of 5.0 × 10−10 mol/L and a limit of quantification of 1.0 × 10−9 mol/L.

Yu et al. synthesized dendritic Au–Ag bimetallic nanoparticles which reacted with hydrogen peroxide that was generated by the enzymatic oxidation between d-glucose and glucose oxidase producing a strong CL emission catalysed by HRP as shown in Fig. 11 [96]. The unique and well-defined dendritic morphology of the bimetallic NPS showed excellent CL activity due to covalent interactions between (Au–N and Ag–N) the surface of the NPS and luminol.

Ma et al. determined different concentrations of SMZ was by using the BS-functionalized core/multishell QDs immunoassay, and LOD was observed at 9pgmL–1 and the analytical range was 0.01–50ngmL–1 [97]. The LOD of the assay was far below the maximum residue limit of SMZ (100ngmL–1). Quenching effect of the SMZ is due to the inhibition of the binding between BS-QDs and mAb-HRP and hence blocking the CRET process.

Some major disadvantages of current CL systems include high-temperature processing, use of expensive and hazardous chemicals along with complex synthesis techniques which seriously hampers their application in various analytical applications. Hence to develop more green and ecofriendly catalysts are highly desirable. Wang et al. used sepiolite, a natural clay mineral for catalyzing luminol–H2O2 system [98]. It was found that due to distinct hierarchical architecture, periodic structure, pore size, high adsorption capacity and large specific area can drastically enhance CL intensity. Natural clay kaolin was used recently in a novel ONOOH-calcein modified kaolin CL system by Wang et al. [99]. The structural modification of kaolin resulted in formation of mullite phase having multihole molecular sieve like structure. The increased specific surface area and the p-type zeolite amplified the weak CL intensity. This method was satisfactorily applied to detect calcein (a fluorescent dye) in water samples.

Layered double hydroxide (LDH) nanoparticles are hydrotalcite like compounds comprising of lamellar hydroxide having two types of metallic cations in the main layer and anions contained in interlayer domains [100]. Due to their interesting structure chromophores can be attached through intercalation, adsorption or other methods and hence the optical characteristics of the chromophore can be enhanced. Wang et al. were the first to employ catalytic property of interlayer carbonate in Mg–Al layered double hydroxides in luminol based CL system [101].

Montmorillonite (MMT) is common clay belonging to the host–guest minerals. The structure of MMT differs from LDHs in having anionic sheets and cationic gallery ions. Organically modified MMT uphold the functional cationic head groups and discard the halide counterions, thus leading to great enhancement of CL. Zhou et al. utilized the MMT nanosheet colloids to enhance hydrogen peroxide ultra-weak chemiluminescence [102]. Recently Zhong et al. reviewed layered-nanomaterial-amplified CL systems based on graphene and its derivatives, layered double hydroxides, and clay [103].

Wang et al. fabricated Mg–Al–CO3 LDHs nanosheets that could serve as catalyst as well as CRET acceptor leading to CL enhancement in bis (2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (TCPO)–H2O2 CL system [104]. On the basis of their ability to quench the CL of TCPO–H2O2, a turn-off CL probe was fabricated for detecting seven biogenic amines (including cholamine, histamine, tyramine, spermine, spermidine, 2-phenylethylamine and putrescine). It was found that the displacement of the intermolecular O–H⋯O bonding between formamide and LDH nanosheets by O–H⋯N bonding between biogenic amines and LDH nanosheets give rise to CL switching behavior. This study not only provided a feasible method for hydrogen bonding-induced inactivation of CL but also provides a detailed understanding of the essence of LDH nanosheet-amplified CL.

ROS are reactive molecules and free radicals that are derived from molecular oxygen. Several CL based systems have been developed due to their high sensitivity as compared to other absorbance based detection method [105]. Nanomaterials due to their interesting properties have been extensively utilized as optical labels for biosensing and biorecognition. In conventional CL systems the use of high concentrations of fluorescent dyes may lead to quenching due to inevitable aggregation, hence decreasing the CRET efficiency. One of the major challenges in detecting ROS is development of semiconductor QD based luminescent probes as many of the ROS are strong quenchers of the CL intensity. Very recently Wang et al. studied the ROS viz. O2 •-, •OH and H2O2 generated from UV irradiation of the semiconductor nanoparticle suspension. The metal-oxide NPs used in this method includes TiO2, nZnO, nCeO2, nV2O5, nFe2O3 and nAl2O3 [106]. The result suggested that the reducing power of the conduction band plays an important role in the formation of O2 •-. Considering these facts it can be inferred that tuning the electronic structure and redox potentials of the nanoparticles, generation of ROS can be monitored through CL. It was found that the selectivity and sensitivity of a specific ROS in these three different methods was very high. This study can open doors for studying the reaction kinetics of decomposition of environmental contaminants using ROS. Recently Zhou et al. reported a QD-based turn-on luminescent probe for the specific detection of ˙ OH [107]. The turn-on luminescent probe for. OH species comprised of metal citrate complexes on the surface of CdTeQDs. These complexes inject electrons into the LUMO of the CdTe QDs while the. OH species act as electron acceptors, injecting holes into the HOMO of the CdTe QDs and their recombination results in the formation of excited state QDs, which can generate strong luminescence by electron-transfer recombination. The system was successfully applied in monitoring the endogenous release of ˙ OH species in living cells. The specific selectivity of the probe for ˙ OH radical and no response to other ROS has made it interesting area of exploration. In another work Zhou reported the specific detection of peroxynitrite (ONOO–) in living cells. The results indicated that the interaction of QDs with oxidizing/reducing ROS radicals can generate a strong CL emission by electron-transfer annihilation [108].

In conventional CL mechanism the emission of light is explained on the basis of oxidation of CL reagents by ROS but to identify the primary radical responsible for emission is still a great challenge. Hence it is difficult to reveal the intrinsic mechanism of CL and finding the new CL emitter. Generally in luminol based CL reaction, it is necessary to add oxidants or regulate pH in alkaline condition. However Sun et al. reported very recently that injection of Au NCs into luminol produced CL without excitation or use of oxidant/alkaline media [109]. On the basis of experimental and computational data radical-mediated spin-transfer on Au NCs was suggested as possible mechanism. O2 •- was identified as primary radical and it facilitated the spin-transfer of 3O2 to aminobenzene ring of luminol assisted by Au NCs. This method due to its interesting properties and mechanism can open new opportunities in protein discrimination and exploring the role of nanoclusters in other CL systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CL in nanomaterial characterization
ce:section.ce:para: In general the proposed mechanism for CL based on luminol–H2O2 in alkaline medium due to some ROS viz. O2 •− and OH•. However the metal NP catalyzed CL mechanism does not involve singlet oxygen (1O2) induced CL. Wang et al. suggested an entirely new mechanism based on generation of 1O2 in GO catalyzed luminol– H2O2 [110]. In another article Wang et al. studied the catalytic activity of cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide-passivated carbon nanodots (CTAB–CDs) on the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction [74]. The results of this study suggested that the surface positive charge density of CTAB-CDs played a vital role in superior catalytic activity due to attachment of anionic luminol or HO2 − on the CTAB-CDs through the electrostatic interaction. This might resulted in increased local concentration of luminol or HO2 −, thus accelerating CL. In explaining the CL mechanism of the ONOOH-calcein-modified kaolin CL Wang et al. suggested that there are abundant oxygen vacancies and active sites on the mullite and P-type zeolite composite phases in the modified kaolin, which lead to the more efficient CRET and stronger CL intensity [99]. Oxygen-states (O-states) are surface states related to oxygenous functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as C–O, C=O, O–C=O. To understand the mechanism of the catalytic activity and quantum yield of CDs based CL reaction the detail studies of theses surface states become essential. Very recently Dong et al. reported that the ONOO−-induced CL of S doped CDs. It was found that intensity was proportional to the content of C–O group-related O-states in S-CDs [111]. These studies reveal the most straightforward application of CL technique in on-line and in-situ monitoring of basic sites, evaluating oxygen vacancies and oxygen in different nanomaterials.

The literature survey so far covered suggests that low quantum yield in CL produced from reactions of inorganic molecules is a major challenge and hence for analytical application new CL systems must be developed. Nanomaterials have shown promising application as futuristic materials as CL emitter/catalyst but the conventional QDs can be modified for more better and novel properties. Doping these QDs with transition metal ions/rare earth ions can drastically tune the electronic and optical properties and subsequently help in enhancing the CL intensity. Conventional semiconductor QDs/Metal NPs involves heavy metals which are hazardous and thus have toxicity concerns in living system, so there is a need to discover more environmental friendly and biocompatible QDs. For many nanomaterials, hydrophobicity and easily aggregation in the water are all-important challenges in the practical application. The research must now be focused on finding new CL emitters in which QDs can be functionalized by novel biomolecules through different methods like physical adsorption, electrostatic interaction, covalent binding and specific affinity interaction etc. The other major area that needs to be focused is ultrasensitivity and quantification in detection of analytes specially the biomolecules. Nanomaterials in various forms such as nanotubes, nanowires, and hollow spheres can be fabricated and tuned (in terms of size and shape) with respect to the specific need and can be excellent candidates in improving the analytical performances of the CL based assay. These improved nanomaterials might have high extinction coefficient, strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR), size- and shape-dependent optical properties that will be future of nanomaterials assisted CL assays. The use of nanocarbon materials like graphene is gaining exponential importance due their unique features such as outstanding electron transfer rate, high quenching efficiency, ultra large planar structure. Most of the work done so far on graphene material based Chemiluminescence enhanced assays includes detecting metal ions, markers for tumors, DNA and other small molecules. Chen et al. have provided a comprehensive review on graphene material based CL reaction [112].

CRET based analytical application are still limited due to poor energy-transfer efficiency and limited number of energy acceptors in the conventional CL systems involving CRET. Novel CRET systems with superior catalytic properties must be developed by adjusting the surface functionalities of these nanomaterials.

Besides studying graphene enhanced CL properties of the conventional luminol-H2O2 system new CL systems must be studied to provide an in depth utilization of these novel nanomaterials. Since graphene itself has no band gap properties they can be modified by incorporating with high band gap materials and that will definitely bring forth drastic results in future CL assays [113]. Studies on 2D carbon material such as graphene or GO and other 0D carbonaceous nanodots suggested that they can act as super-quencher of a CL donor or fluorophore in CRET. Magnetic nanoparticles are also offering promising application in immunoassay and hybridization labels in clinical and pharmaceutical, environmental, food analysis and other allied fields. These systems have been applied in diverse fields in both research and commercial fields, such as clinical chemistry [114]. For analytical application in CL doping carbon dots with N and increasing the N/C atomic ratio is highly desirable as it modify the properties of C-QDS mainly in increasing the quantum yield. Zheng reported very recently that the ultra-weak chemiluminescent reaction of NaIO4 with H2O2 was catalyzed by novel nitrogen quantum dots with a very high percentage of nitrogen (57%) with a 400 fold increase in the ultraweak chemiluminescent reaction of NaIO4 [115]. The interesting results suggest that novel quantum dots based on the nitrogen element will be utilized in several fields in future. The biological systems are extremely versatile hence it is not possible to develop a general biosensor. Detection of each specific analyte requires a specific biosensor. Optical and electronic properties of QDs might be further exploited to design sensitive multiplexed biosensors. As far as bioconjugation of nanomaterials is concerned certain issues are still prevalent such as agglomeration, stabilization due to pH, toxicity and large size of the biomolecules. Moreover the integration of QDs in real life clinical application is a big challenge specially in generating reproducible nanoparticle bioconjugates. The sensitivity in detection and LOD of certain chemicals is a growing concern in food safety to prevent the risk of human exposure. The detection method based on nanosized materials can be an excellent alternative to expensive techniques like liquid chromatography– mass spectrometry (LC–MS). The other important issue in detection of potential carcinogens in contaminated food/agricultural products is recovery with better detection limits. These nanomaterials based CL assay will serve as inexpensive, more efficient system in future for detection of toxins, antibiotics etc. in food materials.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Layered Double Hydroxides: Present and Future
